coredata.dc:title: Glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters mediated-inner filter effect for sensitive and selective determination of p-nitrophenol and alkaline phosphatase activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hai-Bo
Tao, Bei-Bei
Wu, Ning-Ning
Zhang, Hong-Ding
Liu, Yan-Ming
coredata.dc:description: A simple and highly selective fluorescence biosensor has been exploited for p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity detection based on the glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) mediated-inner filter effect (IFE). The GSH-CuNCs were prepared by employing GSH as stabilizer and ascorbic acid (AA) as reductant. The obtained GSH-CuNCs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence emission at 420 nm with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, which overlapped largely with the absorption spectra of p-nitrophenol (p-NP). Therefore, the luminescence of GSH-CuNCs could be quenched by p-NP through inner filter effect. In addition, ALP catalyzed the substrate p-nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP) to form p-nitrophenol (p-NP), which also leading to the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs. The fluorescent strategy was realized for the sensitive determination of p-NP and ALP activity with the promising limit of detection of 20 nM (for p-NP) and 0.003 mU⋅mL−1 (for ALP). Furthermore, the method could be applied to detect the p-NP content in river water samples and ALP activity in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters
P-nitrophenol detection
Alkaline phosphatase activity
Inner filter effect
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for p-NP assay in real water samples
Design principle of the fluorimetric bioassay
Mechanism of fluorescent assay towards p-NP
p-NP assay in river water samples
Determination of ALP in human serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of GSH-CuNCs. (B) Size distribution histogram of GSH-CuNCs. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of GSH-CuNCs. (D) Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of GSH-CuNCs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs (a), GSH-CuNCs + 200 μM p-NPP (b), GSH-CuNCs + 200 μM p-NP (c), and GSH-CuNCs + 200 μM p-NPP + 50 mU⋅mL−1 ALP (d). (B) Fluorescence excitation spectra of GSH-CuNCs (a), fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs (b), and UV–Vis absorption spectra of p-NP (c).

(A) The effect of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs in the absence (a) and presence of 200 μM p-NP (b). (B) The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs in the absence (a) and presence of 200 μM p-NP (b).
(A) The effect of pH value on the fluorescence responses (F 0 - F) toward ALP assay. (B) The effect of incubation time of ALP on the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs in the presence of different concentrations of p-NP. (B) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm and the p-NP concentrations. (C) The calibration plots between fluorescence responses (F 0/F) and p-NP concentrations.

(A) The selectivity of GSH-CuNCs based sensor against 100 μM of analogs and other various compounds. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of 100 μM of o-NP (a), m-NP (b), and p-NP (c) in Tris-HCl buffer solutions (pH 8.0).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the strategy in the presence of increasing ALP concentrations. The curves from a to m, the concentration of ALP are 0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 mU⋅mL−1, respectively. (B) The calibration plots between fluorescence responses (F 0/F) and ALP concentrations.
Fluorescence responses of the ALP assay against the interfering enzymes or proteins.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescent assay for p-NP and ALP activity detection based on GSH-CuNCs and inner filter effect.
Comparison of various fluorescent nanomaterials based methods for p-NP detection.
Determination of p-NP contents in Shi River water samples (n = 3).
Comparison of various fluorescent nanomaterials based methods for ALP determination.
Determination of ALP activity in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters mediated-inner filter effect for sensitive and selective determination of p-nitrophenol and alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains, Xinyang Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterials for Bioanalysis, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains
Xinyang Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterials for Bioanalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains, Xinyang Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterials for Bioanalysis, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive and selective fluorescent biosensor has been exploited for the determination of p-NP and ALP activity based on the GSH-CuNCs mediated-inner filter effect.

A simple and highly selective fluorescence biosensor has been exploited for p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity detection based on the glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) mediated-inner filter effect (IFE). The GSH-CuNCs were prepared by employing GSH as stabilizer and ascorbic acid (AA) as reductant. The obtained GSH-CuNCs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence emission at 420 nm with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, which overlapped largely with the absorption spectra of p-nitrophenol (p-NP). Therefore, the luminescence of GSH-CuNCs could be quenched by p-NP through inner filter effect. In addition, ALP catalyzed the substrate p-nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP) to form p-nitrophenol (p-NP), which also leading to the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs. The fluorescent strategy was realized for the sensitive determination of p-NP and ALP activity with the promising limit of detection of 20 nM (for p-NP) and 0.003 mU⋅mL−1 (for ALP). Furthermore, the method could be applied to detect the p-NP content in river water samples and ALP activity in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As a type of refractory pollutant, p-nitrophenol (p-NP) was mainly derived from its widespread applicability in the fields of the agriculture and industry process, such as pesticides, pharmaceuticals, explosives, dyes and so on [1]. It was well known that p-nitrophenol (p-NP) had caused serious threat not only to human health but also to plants, animals and aquatic organism, due to its low biodegradability and high toxicity [1–3]. Because it was still very difficult to eliminate the p-NP contents from the polluted agriculture crops (e.g. fruits, vegetables) and water resources, owing to its high solubility in water and poor degradability [4–6]. United States (U. S.) Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) had established a maximum allowable level of p-NP contents in drinking water no more than 60 μg/L (∼430 nM) [4]. Thus, it was crucial to explore highly sensitive strategy for the monitoring the p-NP in real water samples, especially distinguishing p-NP among the coexisting isomers (o-NP and m-NP).

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was an enzyme widely found in various mammal tissues including intestine, liver, bone, kidney, placenta and so on. The ALP level was not only responsible for the dephosphorylation of small molecules, nucleic acids and proteins in living organisms, but also played a vital role in signal transduction and regulation of cell growth and apoptosis [7,8]. As an important indicator, ALP was usually regarded as one of the most widely used biomarkers for the clinical diagnosis and treatment of certain diseases [9]. The abnormal serum ALP levels were implicated in the development of many illnesses, such as breast, prostate cancer, liver dysfunction and diabetes [10,11]. Therefore, it was essential to develop highly sensitive strategy for the detection of the ALP activity contents in human serum samples.

Up to date, a number of analytical methods have been established for the monitoring of ALP activity, including colorimetric methods, fluorescent methods, electrochemical methods, chromatography, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, chemiluminescence and electrochemiluminescence methods [12–18]. However, many of them generally suffered from some disadvantages such as time-consuming, high cost, complex synthesis process and surface modification. Compared with other methods, fluorescent strategies have the merits of high sensitivity, simple operation, fast response speed, high reliability, and real-time analysis [19–22]. Thus, fluorescent methods for ALP activity assay have attracted most of attentions.

In recent years, several fluorescent sensing platforms had been constructed for ALP activity detection [23–27]. However, most of these methods depended on the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) induced fluorescence quenching. As the PET or FRET based fluorescence quenching efficiency relied on the distance between the acceptor and the donor, these methods usually required chemical conjugation or surface modification on the acceptor or the donor. In contrast, the inner filter effect (IFE) mediated fluorescent method was a simple and powerful strategy for detection of ALP activity, because IFE could eliminate environmental interference and background noises efficiently. More importantly, IFE-based methods did not need any surface modification or covalent linking between absorbers and fluorophores [28–30]. The IFE process occurred while the absorption spectrum of the absorbers overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum or fluorescence emission spectrum of fluorophores, only involving the absorption of an absorber to the excitation and/or emission of light. By translating the absorption information into fluorescent responses, the sensitivity and selectivity of IFE-based methods were improved in comparison with PET and FRET based fluorescence quenching mechanisms [31–34].

Recently, some fluorescent nanomaterials (such as CdTe/CdS QDs, carbon dots, Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots, gold nanoclusters, silicon nanoparticles) IFE-based sensing platforms have been constructed for the ALP activity assay [35–38]. However, the semiconductor (CdTe/CdS) quantum dots had the disadvantages of the high toxicity. The preparation of carbon dots (180 °C for 12 h) and silicon nanoparticles (200 °C for 4 h) required long high-temperature treatment. The MXene quantum dots commonly suffered from high-cost and complex preparation. The preparation of gold nanoclusters was very time-consuming (over 72 h). Therefore, the exploration of low-toxic and favorable luminescent nanomaterials to design and develop IFE-based fluorescent sensor was still highly desirable and challenging.

In this work, glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) were synthesized through facile one-step green approach by employing GSH as stabilizer and AA as reductant. The obtained GSH-CuNCs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence emission at 420 nm with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, which overlapped well with the absorption spectra of p-NP. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs could be quenched by p-NP through IFE. On the other hand, by utilization of p-NPP as the ALP substrate, p-NPP could be hydrolyzed to p-NP, leading to the fluorescence decreasing of GSH-CuNCs. Based on this principle, a highly selective fluorescence biosensing platform has been designed for the determination of p-NP and ALP activity based on GSH-CuNCs and inner filter effect.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glutathione (GSH), thrombin, acetylcholinesterase (AChE), glucose oxidase (GOx), peroxidase from horseradish (HRP), lysozyme, and trypsin were purchased from Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), p-nitrophenylphosphate (p-NPP), o-nitrophenol (o-NP), m-nitrophenol (m-NP), p-nitrophenol (p-NP), 2, 4, 6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP), ascorbic acid (AA) and other chemicals (analytical grade) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water were used in the whole process of experiment with an electric resistance greater than 18.2 MΩ•cm.

The fluorescent measurements were performed by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Japan). The fluorescence emission spectrum was collected from 390 nm to 600 nm with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was carried out on a UH-4150 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected from Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN field emission transmission electron microscopy.

GSH-stabilized CuNCs (GSH-CuNCs) were synthesized through previous reported works with slight modification [39]. In brief, 125 μL of 50 mM CuSO4·5H2O solutions were added into 40 mL of 0.21 mM freshly-prepared GSH solutions under vigorous stirring. The mixture became turbid gradually, because the copper thiolate complexes were formed. After being stirring for 5 min, 900 μL of 100 mM freshly-prepared AA solutions were added, and sequentially incubated at 65 °C for 4 h under stirring. The mixture became clear slowly. After that, the mixture cooled down to room temperature. The product was treated by centrifugation at 16 000 rpm for 10 min. At last, the supernatant was obtained and placed at 4 °C for further experiment.

In the p-NP assay, 20 μL of GSH-CuNCs, 20 μL of Tris-HCl solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0), 10 μL of p-NP (different concentrations), were added into 100 μL sensing system and incubated for 1 min at room temperature. After that, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were performed immediately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for p-NP assay in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: River water was collected from Shi River in Xinyang City. The water samples were firstly filtered through a 0.22 μm cellulose membrane, and centrifuged by 12 000 rpm before test. Then, different concentrations of standard solutions of p-NP were spiked into the prepared water samples. At last, the fluorescent intensity at 420 nm was immediately got with a fluorescence excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

In the ALP activity assay, 10 μL of 2 mM p-NPP, 20 μL of ultrapure water, 10 μL of ALP (different concentrations), 10 μL of Tris-HCl solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0) were mixed and incubated 37 °C for 40 min. Then, 20 μL of GSH-CuNCs, 20 μL of ultrapure water, and 10 μL of Tris-HCl solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0) were added into the mixture. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the solution was collected from 390 nm to 600 nm immediately.

Human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers provided by the Hospital of Xinyang Normal University. The serum samples were centrifuged by 10 000 rpm for 10 min and diluted appropriately with Tris-HCl solutions. In real sample assay, the ALP solution was replaced with a serum sample and other procedures were carried out as in the above ALP activity assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of the fluorimetric bioassay
ce:section.ce:para: The working principle of the fluorescent bioassay for p-NP and ALP activity was illustrated in Scheme 1 . GSH-CuNCs with strong blue fluorescence emission were prepared by using GSH as stabilizer and AA as reducing agents. The obtained GSH-CuNCs possessed strong blue emission at 420 nm under an excitation of 365 nm. Interesting, the luminescence of GSH-CuNCs could be quenched obviously by p-NP through inner filter effect (IFE) process. This was because the absorption spectra of p-NP overlapped largely with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs. On the other hand, the ALP could catalyze and hydrolyze the substrate p-NPP to form p-NP under the alkaline condition. The in-situ formation of p-NP could induce fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs by IFE, which was positively related to the ALP activity. As a consequence, the real-time monitoring of ALP activity could be indirectly realized through monitoring the in-situ produced p-NP. On the basis of this principle, a simple fluorescent biosensor has been designed for the highly selective determination of p-NP and ALP activity.

The synthesized GSH-CuNCs was firstly characterized by TEM images. As showed in (Fig. 1 A), the GSH-CuNCs had an approximately spherical shape and uniform size distribution. And the average diameter GSH-CuNCs were estimated to be 3.36 ± 1.49 nm from TEM image analysis (Fig. 1B). Moreover, the optical properties of the GSH-CuNCs were also studied. (Fig. 1C) represented the UV–Vis absorption spectra of GSH-CuNCs dispersed in water. It was observed that there was an intense absorption at 293 nm (UV region). However, no obvious absorption was found over 400 nm (visible region). It suggested that GSH-CuNCs possessed molecule-like properties and did not have larger copper nanoparticles, indicating the high purity of GSH-CuNCs. As for (Fig. 1D), it described the GSH-CuNCs displayed an intense fluorescence emission wavelength at 420 nm (curve b) under the maximum excitation wavelength of 365 nm (curve a). And the fluorescence quantum yield of GSH-CuNCs was determined to be 8.5%.

To confirm the feasibility of the fluorescent strategy, the luminescence of GSH-CuNCs were studied in the presence of different substrates. As displayed in (Fig. 2 A) (curve a), the GSH-CuNCs possessed strong blue fluorescence intensity with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As observed in curve b of (Fig. 2A), p-nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP) also slightly quenched the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs. However, under the condition of the same concentrations (200 μM) of p-NP and p-NPP, p-NP could result in a most of fluorescence quenching (curve c of Fig. 2A). When p-NPP and ALP were simultaneously introduced and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, the fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs decreased obviously (curve d of Fig. 2A), which was similar to the addition of the same concentration of p-NP to GSH-CuNCs directly (curve c). It might be due to the ALP catalyzed p-NPP to form p-NP and the in-situ formation of p-NP caused fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs. These results suggested that the fluorescence assay was feasible for detecting p-NP and ALP activity. UV–Vis absorption spectrum of free p-NP under alkaline condition was measured. As presented in (Fig. 2B), p-NP possessed a strong absorption peak at 405 nm (curve c), which was largely overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra (curve a) and fluorescence emission spectra (curve b) of GSH-CuNCs. As a result, the p-NP induced-fluorescence quenching might be attributed to the IFE process.

In p-NP assay, some key factors such as pH values and incubation time of p-NP, have been investigated to obtain the good analytical performance. (Fig. 3 A) (curve a) showed the luminescence of GSH-CuNCs was no obvious changed with pH values increasing from 6.0 to 9.0, suggesting that the GSH-CuNCs possessed good stability. However, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs in the presence of p-NP declined quickly as pH values increased (curve b in Fig. 3A). Because the absorption peak of p-NP occurred an obvious bathochromic shift from 320 nm to 400 nm with the increase of pH values from 6.0 to 9.0. And a remarkable fluorescence quenching was observed and arrived at a maximum when pH value was 8.0. This is because the bathochromic shift of absorption peak of p-NP caused the increasing of the overlap degrees between the absorption spectrum of p-NP and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of GSH-CuNCs. Therefore, pH 8.0 was selected for the p-NP assay. The incubation time of p-NP was another key factor for p-NP assay. From (Fig. 3B), it was observed that the luminescence of GSH-CuNCs was quenched by p-NP within 1 min and no obvious changes after 1 min, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching equilibrium was very rapid.

In the ALP activity assay, the effect of the pH values of buffer solutions and incubation time of ALP were investigated to obtain better performance for ALP activity detection. (Fig. 4 A) illustrated the fluorescent responses (F 0 - F) under various pH values of buffer solutions. It was noted that there was significant fluorescent responses at alkaline condition. And the maximum fluorescence changes were observed while pH value of buffer solutions was 8.0. Therefore, pH 8.0 was preferred in this fluorescent assay. Furthermore, incubation time of ALP was also critical for detecting the ALP activity in the fluorescent sensing strategy. As depicted in (Fig. 4B), the fluorescence intensity decreased dramatically and reached the equilibrium after 40 min. The results demonstrated that p-NPP was converted to p-NP completely by ALP after 40 min.

As indicated in (Fig. 5 A), the fluorescence emission intensity of GSH-CuNCs declined along with p-NP concentrations increased from 0 μM to 300 μM. (Fig. 5B) displayed the plots of the relationship of the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm versus the p-NP concentrations. When 200 μM of p-NP was present, 86% of the fluorescence intensity (at 420 nm) of GSH-CuNCs was quenched. As displayed in (Fig. 5C), fluorescence responses (F 0/F) possessed a good linear relationship against the p-NP concentrations from 100 nM to 150 μM (R = 0.9989). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 20 nM, which was less than the standard of U. S. EPA (no more than 430 nM) [3]. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated to be 70 nM. Furthermore, the LOD was lower than or comparable to many reported fluorescent nanomaterials-based methods (Table 1 ) [2–6].

In addition, the selectivity of the assay for p-NP assay was evaluated by testing the fluorescence responses (F 0/F) of other potential interference compounds, especially some isomers with the same molecular formula (such as o-NP and m-NP). As shown in Fig. 6 A, these interference compounds did not result in obvious fluorescence responses except for p-NP. More importantly, it could selectively distinguish p-NP from the coexisting isomers (o-NP and m-NP). Because there was little overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectrum of isomers (o-NP and m-NP) and the fluorescence excitation spectra or fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs (Fig. 6B). It was further supported that p-NP induced fluorescence quenching was attribute to inner filter effect (IFE). As a result, it suggested that the method had a good selectivity toward p-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescent assay towards p-NP
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching, a series of control experiments were performed. Firstly, the effect of the isomers compounds (including p-NP, o-NP and m-NP) on the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs was measured. However, o-NP and m-NP only caused a negligible fluorescence quenching towards GSH-CuNCs (shown in Fig. 6A). In addition, the zeta potential of GSH-CuNCs without and with p-NP was −30.5 mV and –33.8 mV, respectively. It suggested that there was electrostatic repulsion between the fluorescent p-NP and GSH-CuNCs. Thus, electron transfer (ET) was not the dominant quenching mechanism. Secondly, the TEM images of GSH-CuNCs in the presence of p-NP were also studied. As shown in Fig. S1, it was observed that the shape and the average size of GSH-CuNCs were no obvious changes, suggesting that the introduction of p-NP had no effects on the morphology of GSH-CuNCs. Additionally, time-resolved decay curves of GSH-CuNCs with and without p-NP were investigated (shown in Fig. S2). It was observed that the fluorescence lifetimes change was very little with the addition of p-NP. Taking these results together, it confirmed that the p-NP mediated fluorescence quenching mainly attributed to the IFE process [4,28,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: p-NP assay in river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence method was also applied for the p-NP assay in Shi River water samples. As given in Table 2 , there was no p-NP content was found in the water samples. As a result, standard addition method was utilized through spiking standard solutions of different concentrations of p-NP. The recoveries were from 98.0 to 103.4% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) no more than 4.2% (n = 3), which suggested that the strategy had the potential application in detecting p-NP contents in river water samples.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, ALP activity determination was investigated by collecting the fluorescent responses of the GSH-CuNCs in the presence of enzyme substrate (p-NPP). Fig. 7A depicted that the fluorescence intensity of sensing system decreased gradually with a slight red shift as the ALP concentrations increased from 0 to 50 mU⋅mL−1 (curve a to curve m). It means that with the increasing concentrations of ALP, the concentrations of in-situ p-NP increased due to the hydrolysis of p-NPP catalyzed by ALP. As displayed in Fig. 7B, a good linear relationship (R = 0.9937) was established between the fluorescent responses (F 0/F) and ALP concentrations in the range from 0.01 to 40 mU⋅mL−1. The fluorescence intensity of sensing strategy at 420 nm in the existence or absence of ALP was denoted by F and F 0, respectively. The LOD was worked out to be 0.003 mU⋅mL−1 (S/N = 3), which was lower than or comparable to some reported fluorescent methods (Table 3 ) [27,34–37]. In addition, the LOQ was calculated to be 0.01 mU⋅mL−1.

In order to study the selectivity of this assay for ALP activity, the effects of other enzymes or proteins on the fluorescence intensity have been evaluated, such as trypsin, GOx, BSA, lysozyme, thrombin, AChE, HRP. As indicated in Fig. 8 , these interfering enzymes or proteins did not cause significant fluorescence decreasing compared with the blank solution. However, the fluorescence intensity was obviously quenched with the introduction of ALP. The results suggested that this assay had good selectivity for ALP assay, which might be ascribed to the specific catalytic reaction of ALP on p-NPP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALP in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: The biosensing strategy was also applied for the detection of ALP activity in 50-fold diluted human serum samples. As listed in Table 4 , the recoveries were ranging from 98.1 to 102.2% and the RSD was no more than 4.2% (n = 3), which confirmed this strategy could be used to determinate the ALP activity in human serum samples.

In conclusion, a simple and reliable fluorescent strategy had been developed for the p-NP and ALP activity determination by employing GSH-CuNCs as the fluorescent nanoprobes. The fluorescent assay was relied on the fluorescence quenching caused by the internal filter effect (IFE) between GSH-CuNCs and p-NP. The assay was simple and convenient, less sample consumption and no modification. More importantly, the method possessed a much higher sensitivity compared with the traditional fluorescent assay for p-NP and ALP activity. This strategy opened up a new way for the detection of p-NP and ALP activity, and widened the potential application of metal nanoclusters.

Hai-Bo Wang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Bei-Bei Tao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ning-Ning Wu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Hong-Ding Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Yan-Ming Liu: Resources, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1704153), 1000 Talents Program of Zhongyuan (ZYQR201912153), High-level Talent in Henan Province (202101JY0940C), Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of XYNU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.120948.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Talanta

J. Hazard. Mater.

Microchim. Acta
